name: Portugal
summary: Portugal is known for indigenous grape varieties and diverse wine styles, ranging from fresh Atlantic whites to powerful inland reds.
examTips: Portugal relies heavily on indigenous grape varieties.
Atlantic vs inland climate is the key concept.
Douro is important for both dry wines and Port.
Madeira is the other key fortified wine name to remember, while Bairrada is a useful Atlantic Baga and sparkling anchor.

vinho-verde.name: Vinho Verde
vinho-verde.summary: Cool, Atlantic-influenced region producing light, fresh, high-acid white wines with low alcohol.
vinho-verde.styleSummary: Light, fresh, high-acid wines with low alcohol.
examTips: Very high acidity and low alcohol.
Sometimes slight spritz.

Alvarinho.summary: High-quality aromatic white grape producing citrus and stone fruit wines with good structure.
viticulture: Better sites give more body
examTips: More structured than other Vinho Verde wines.

Loureiro.summary: Aromatic grape producing light, floral wines.
viticulture: Cool climate
examTips: Very light and aromatic.

moncao-e-melgaco.name: Moncao e Melgaco
moncao-e-melgaco.summary: Prestige inland Vinho Verde sub-region best known for higher-quality Alvarinho.
moncao-e-melgaco.styleSummary: More concentrated aromatic dry whites with citrus, peach and high acidity.
examTips: This is the key Alvarinho-focused Vinho Verde sub-region.
Often fuller and more concentrated than broader regional blends.

Alvarinho.summary: High-quality aromatic white grape producing citrus and stone fruit wines with good structure.
viticulture: Better sites give more body
examTips: More structured than other Vinho Verde wines.

vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Rainfall and humidity raise disease pressure and can make ripening more variable.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Canopy management and disease control are especially important where humidity is high.
Regional style depends not only on climate and site, but also on deliberate human choices in the vineyard and cellar.

lima.name: Lima
lima.summary: Vinho Verde sub-region associated strongly with Loureiro-based aromatic whites.
lima.styleSummary: Light aromatic whites with floral citrus character and brisk acidity.
examTips: Loureiro is the grape to connect here.
Think freshness and aromatic lift.

Loureiro.summary: Aromatic grape producing light, floral wines.
viticulture: Cool climate
examTips: Very light and aromatic.

vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Rainfall and humidity raise disease pressure and can make ripening more variable.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Canopy management and disease control are especially important where humidity is high.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

vinho-verde.classification: Vinho Verde is a broad DOC associated with fresh high-acid wines, with Moncao e Melgaco especially important for Alvarinho.
lawNotes: Broader regional wines are often blends, while top Alvarinho examples may come from Moncao e Melgaco.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Rainfall and humidity raise disease pressure and can make ripening more variable.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Canopy management and disease control are especially important where humidity is high.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

douro.name: Douro
douro.summary: Hot inland region producing powerful dry reds and the fortified wines of Port from steep terraced vineyards.
douro.styleSummary: Structured dry reds plus sweet fortified Port in a range of oxidative and bottle-aged styles.
examTips: Douro is important for both dry wines and Port.
Steep terraced vineyards and hot inland conditions are key themes.
Fortification stops fermentation and preserves sweetness in Port.

Touriga Nacional.summary: Portugal’s flagship red grape, producing concentrated colour, tannin and floral perfume in dry Douro wines and Port blends.
viticulture: Low yields
winemaking: Also used for premium dry reds
examTips: Highly structured and concentrated.
Central grape in many top Port blends.

Touriga Franca.summary: Perfumed blending grape adding fruit and balance in Port and dry Douro wines.
viticulture: Reliable
winemaking: Useful in blends for Port and dry reds
examTips: Reliable and widely planted.
Often adds perfume and approachability around Touriga Nacional.

Tinta Roriz.summary: Portuguese name for Tempranillo, used in Port blends and structured dry reds.
Tinta Roriz.style: Red and black fruit with spice and structure.
viticulture: Adapts well to warm dry inland climates
examTips: Know that Tinta Roriz is Tempranillo.
Useful in both fortified and still wines.

ruby.name: Ruby Port
ruby.style: Deeply coloured youthful Port with black fruit and simple sweetness.

reserve-ruby.name: Reserve Ruby Port
reserve-ruby.style: Richer more intense Ruby style with greater concentration.
reserve-ruby.aging: Large-vessel ageing with more selection and quality

lbv.name: LBV Port
lbv.style: Ripe, rich Port from a single year, usually more approachable than Vintage Port.
lbv.aging: Longer cask ageing than Vintage before release
lbv.keyPoint: Single-vintage style designed to be ready earlier.

vintage.name: Vintage Port
vintage.style: Powerful, concentrated and ageworthy Port from the best years.
vintage.aging: Short cask ageing then long bottle ageing
vintage.keyPoint: Declared only in the strongest vintages.

tawny.name: Tawny Port
tawny.style: Nutty, caramel and dried-fruit style with oxidative complexity.
tawny.aging: Extended ageing in small oak vessels
tawny.keyPoint: Oxidation and evaporation shape the style.

aged-tawny.name: 10/20/30/40 Year Tawny Port
aged-tawny.style: Increasingly complex oxidative Port with nut, toffee and dried-fruit character.
aged-tawny.keyPoint: Age indication reflects average style rather than a single vintage.

white-port.name: White Port
white-port.style: Fortified white style ranging from dry to sweet, often served chilled.
white-port.aging: Can be youthful or oxidatively aged depending on style
white-port.keyPoint: Made from white grapes, not just a pale version of ruby.

baixo-corgo.name: Baixo Corgo
baixo-corgo.summary: Cooler wetter western Douro sub-region producing lighter styles and some Port components.
baixo-corgo.styleSummary: Generally lighter less concentrated wines than the central and eastern Douro.
examTips: This is the coolest and wettest Douro sub-region.
Quality and concentration generally increase eastward.

Touriga Nacional.summary: Portugal’s flagship red grape, producing concentrated colour, tannin and floral perfume in dry Douro wines and Port blends.
viticulture: Low yields
winemaking: Also used for premium dry reds
examTips: Highly structured and concentrated.
Central grape in many top Port blends.

Touriga Franca.summary: Perfumed blending grape adding fruit and balance in Port and dry Douro wines.
viticulture: Reliable
winemaking: Useful in blends for Port and dry reds
examTips: Reliable and widely planted.
Often adds perfume and approachability around Touriga Nacional.

Tinta Roriz.summary: Portuguese name for Tempranillo, used in Port blends and structured dry reds.
Tinta Roriz.style: Red and black fruit with spice and structure.
viticulture: Adapts well to warm dry inland climates
examTips: Know that Tinta Roriz is Tempranillo.
Useful in both fortified and still wines.

baixo-corgo.classification: The coolest and wettest of the main Douro sub-regions.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

cima-corgo.name: Cima Corgo
cima-corgo.summary: Central Douro sub-region widely regarded as the heartland of premium Port production.
cima-corgo.styleSummary: Concentrated powerful red and fortified wines from steep terraced vineyards.
examTips: This is the classic premium Port core.
Steep schist vineyards are a key context clue.

Touriga Nacional.summary: Portugal’s flagship red grape, producing concentrated colour, tannin and floral perfume in dry Douro wines and Port blends.
viticulture: Low yields
winemaking: Also used for premium dry reds
examTips: Highly structured and concentrated.
Central grape in many top Port blends.

Touriga Franca.summary: Perfumed blending grape adding fruit and balance in Port and dry Douro wines.
viticulture: Reliable
winemaking: Useful in blends for Port and dry reds
examTips: Reliable and widely planted.
Often adds perfume and approachability around Touriga Nacional.

Tinta Roriz.summary: Portuguese name for Tempranillo, used in Port blends and structured dry reds.
Tinta Roriz.style: Red and black fruit with spice and structure.
viticulture: Adapts well to warm dry inland climates
examTips: Know that Tinta Roriz is Tempranillo.
Useful in both fortified and still wines.

cima-corgo.classification: The prestige central Douro sub-region and classic premium Port core.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

douro-superior.name: Douro Superior
douro-superior.summary: Hot dry eastern Douro sub-region with increasing importance for both still wine and Port.
douro-superior.styleSummary: Deeply ripe concentrated wines with fuller body and higher alcohol potential.
examTips: This is the hottest and driest Douro section.
Modern still table wine production is especially important here.

Touriga Nacional.summary: Portugal’s flagship red grape, producing concentrated colour, tannin and floral perfume in dry Douro wines and Port blends.
viticulture: Low yields
winemaking: Also used for premium dry reds
examTips: Highly structured and concentrated.
Central grape in many top Port blends.

Touriga Franca.summary: Perfumed blending grape adding fruit and balance in Port and dry Douro wines.
viticulture: Reliable
winemaking: Useful in blends for Port and dry reds
examTips: Reliable and widely planted.
Often adds perfume and approachability around Touriga Nacional.

Tinta Roriz.summary: Portuguese name for Tempranillo, used in Port blends and structured dry reds.
Tinta Roriz.style: Red and black fruit with spice and structure.
viticulture: Adapts well to warm dry inland climates
examTips: Know that Tinta Roriz is Tempranillo.
Useful in both fortified and still wines.

douro-superior.classification: The hottest driest Douro sub-region, increasingly important for still wine as well as Port.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.

Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

douro.classification: Douro covers both Port production and an important still table-wine region, with Baixo Corgo, Cima Corgo and Douro Superior as the major sub-zones.
lawNotes: Port styles such as Ruby, LBV, Vintage and Tawny are essential exam categories.
Cima Corgo is the classic premium Port heartland.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

dao.name: Dão
dao.summary: Cooler inland region producing elegant, balanced red wines.
examTips: Cooler than Douro.
More elegant wines.

viticulture: Cooler climate
examTips: More elegant than Douro.

serra-da-estrela.name: Serra da Estrela
serra-da-estrela.summary: Mountain influence is central to Dao, and the Serra da Estrela helps protect vineyards while altitude preserves freshness.
serra-da-estrela.styleSummary: Structured but fresh reds and elegant aromatic whites shaped by elevation.
examTips: Altitude and mountain shelter are the key Dao concepts to remember.
Freshness is usually greater than in hotter inland areas.

viticulture: Cooler climate
examTips: More elegant than Douro.

vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

dao.classification: Dao is a DOC shaped by altitude and mountain shelter, giving fresher structure than hotter inland Portuguese regions.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.

bairrada.name: Bairrada
bairrada.summary: Atlantic-influenced central Portuguese region known for high-acid Baga reds and important sparkling wine production.
bairrada.styleSummary: Firm high-acid reds from Baga plus fresh traditional-method sparkling wines and some textural whites.
examTips: Bairrada is the classic Baga region to remember.
Baga gives high acidity and tannin.
Sparkling wine is an important second identity here.

Baga.summary: Late-ripening Portuguese red grape producing deeply colored, high-acid, high-tannin wines that can age well.
Baga.style: Sour cherry, black plum, earth and firm tannin with high acidity.
viticulture: Late ripening and best on well-exposed sites
Humidity can increase disease pressure
winemaking: Can be austere when young if extraction is high
Also used for sparkling rose base wines
examTips: Think high acidity and tannin rather than soft fruity easy-drinking red.
Atlantic climate helps preserve freshness.

Bical.summary: Traditional white grape of Bairrada giving citrus, stone fruit and mineral notes with good acidity.
Bical.style: Fresh medium-bodied white with citrus, stone fruit and mineral detail.
viticulture: Performs well in Atlantic-influenced sites
Can retain freshness even in warmer vintages
winemaking: Used in both still and sparkling wines
examTips: Bical is useful to know as a Bairrada white and sparkling component.

bairrada-espumante.name: Bairrada Espumante
bairrada-espumante.style: Traditional-method sparkling wine with citrus, apple and brisk acidity.
bairrada-espumante.aging: Bottle-fermented with lees ageing, usually focused on freshness and definition
bairrada-espumante.keyPoint: Bairrada is one of Portugal's key sparkling wine zones.

bairrada.classification: Bairrada DOC is the classic Atlantic Baga and sparkling wine appellation of central Portugal.
lawNotes: Bairrada is important for both Baga reds and traditional-method sparkling wine.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Rainfall and humidity raise disease pressure and can make ripening more variable.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Choice of production method and extraction level is a major human factor in regional wine style.
Matching grape variety and site is important in this mixed still-and-sparkling region.

lisboa.name: Lisboa
lisboa.summary: Broad Atlantic coastal region west and north of Lisbon producing fresh whites and balanced reds from many DOC areas.
lisboa.styleSummary: Fresh high-acid whites and moderate-bodied reds shaped by Atlantic breezes and strong regional variation.
examTips: Think Atlantic freshness rather than hot inland power.
Bucelas is the classic Arinto appellation.
Colares is the famous sandy-coastal home of Ramisco.

Arinto.summary: High-acid Portuguese white grape producing citrus-driven wines with strong freshness and aging potential.
Arinto.style: Lemon, apple and mineral notes with high acidity and a dry finish.
viticulture: Retains acidity well in warm climates
Useful in coastal regions where freshness is prized
winemaking: Usually made in dry fresh styles
Lees ageing or oak can add texture in richer examples
examTips: Arinto is one of Portugal's most reliable high-acid white grapes.

Castelao.summary: Traditional Portuguese red grape producing medium-bodied wines with red fruit, herbs and moderate tannin.
Castelao.style: Red cherry, plum, dried herbs and medium body with moderate tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in sandy or warmer coastal sites
Can ripen unevenly if sites are too cool
winemaking: Often blended but can make varietal wines
Oak can add structure and spice
examTips: Castelao is a useful grape to connect with coastal southern Portuguese reds.

bucelas.name: Bucelas
bucelas.summary: Classic white appellation north of Lisbon best known for high-acid Arinto.
bucelas.styleSummary: Fresh dry whites with lemon, apple, mineral notes and high acidity.
examTips: Bucelas is the Arinto appellation to remember in Lisboa.
High acidity is the key style clue.

Arinto.summary: High-acid Portuguese white grape producing citrus-driven wines with strong freshness and aging potential.
Arinto.style: Lemon, apple and mineral notes with high acidity and a dry finish.
viticulture: Retains acidity well in warm climates
Useful in coastal regions where freshness is prized
winemaking: Usually made in dry fresh styles
Lees ageing or oak can add texture in richer examples
examTips: Arinto is one of Portugal's most reliable high-acid white grapes.

bucelas.classification: Bucelas DOC is Lisboa's best-known white appellation and is strongly associated with Arinto.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Harvest timing and yield control are important in shaping precision and intensity.

colares.name: Colares
colares.summary: Historic Atlantic coastal appellation on sandy soils, best known for long-lived Ramisco reds from ungrafted vines.
colares.styleSummary: Pale to medium-coloured reds with high acidity, firm tannin and savory longevity.
examTips: Think sand, Atlantic winds and ungrafted vines.
Ramisco gives surprisingly high acidity and tannin.

Ramisco.summary: Rare Portuguese red grape of Colares producing pale, very high-acid, tannic wines with notable aging potential.
Ramisco.style: Sour red fruit, herbal, saline and savory with high acidity and firm tannin.
viticulture: Traditionally grown in deep coastal sand
Atlantic exposure slows ripening and preserves acidity
winemaking: Long maturation can reveal notable complexity
examTips: Colares is one of Portugal's most distinctive niche appellations.
Do not expect ripe plush fruit.

colares.classification: Colares DOC is a famous Atlantic coastal appellation associated with sandy soils and Ramisco.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Wind can cool vineyards and reduce fungal disease pressure, but can also affect vigor and fruit set.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Training systems, row orientation, and site protection matter where wind is a major factor.
Preserving old ungrafted vineyards is a major human factor behind the region's identity.

lisboa.classification: Lisboa includes many DOCs, with Bucelas and Colares the two classic study anchors.
lawNotes: Colares is the famous sandy coastal home of Ramisco.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Site variation creates a wide range of mesoclimates and styles.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Matching grape variety to the right coastal or inland site is critical in such a varied region.
Training systems and site protection matter where Atlantic wind is a major factor.

setubal.name: Setubal
setubal.summary: Warm maritime region south of Lisbon known for aromatic Moscatel fortified wines and Castelao-based reds from Palmela.
setubal.styleSummary: Sweet aromatic fortified wines plus ripe but balanced reds from sandy peninsula vineyards.
examTips: Moscatel de Setubal is the key fortified wine to remember here.
Palmela is the red appellation linked to Castelao.

Moscatel de Alexandria.summary: Highly aromatic Muscat grape producing sweet fortified wines with floral, grapey and citrus-peel character.
Moscatel de Alexandria.style: Orange blossom, grape, citrus peel and raisin notes in sweet fortified wines.
viticulture: Well suited to warm sunny climates
Can reach high sugar levels while retaining aromatic intensity
winemaking: Maturation adds honeyed and nutty complexity
examTips: Think grapey aromatics and sweetness balanced by spirit and flavor intensity.

Castelao.summary: Traditional Portuguese red grape producing medium-bodied wines with red fruit, herbs and moderate tannin.
Castelao.style: Red cherry, plum, dried herbs and medium body with moderate tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in sandy or warmer coastal sites
Can handle dry warm conditions
winemaking: Often blended but can make varietal wines
Oak can add structure and spice
examTips: Palmela is the key appellation to connect with Castelao.

moscatel-de-setubal.name: Moscatel de Setubal
moscatel-de-setubal.style: Sweet aromatic fortified wine with orange blossom, grape, citrus peel and honeyed spice.
moscatel-de-setubal.aging: Fortified sweet wine often matured to gain nutty and dried-fruit complexity
moscatel-de-setubal.keyPoint: Aromatic Muscat character is the signature clue.

moscatel-de-setubal.name: Moscatel de Setubal
moscatel-de-setubal.summary: Classic fortified-wine appellation for intensely aromatic sweet wines from Moscatel de Alexandria.
moscatel-de-setubal.styleSummary: Sweet aromatic fortified wines with floral, raisined and citrus-peel complexity.
examTips: This is the fortified Muscat appellation to remember in Portugal beyond Port and Madeira.

Moscatel de Alexandria.summary: Highly aromatic Muscat grape producing sweet fortified wines with floral, grapey and citrus-peel character.
Moscatel de Alexandria.style: Orange blossom, grape, citrus peel and raisin notes in sweet fortified wines.
viticulture: Well suited to warm sunny climates
Can reach high sugar levels while retaining aromatic intensity
winemaking: Maturation adds honeyed and nutty complexity
examTips: Think grapey aromatics and sweetness balanced by spirit and flavor intensity.

moscatel-de-setubal.classification: Moscatel de Setubal DOC is the peninsula's hallmark fortified appellation.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Choice of fortification and maturation is a major human factor in final style.

palmela.name: Palmela
palmela.summary: Red appellation of the Setubal peninsula best known for Castelao grown on sandy soils.
palmela.styleSummary: Medium-bodied reds with red fruit, herbs and moderate tannin.
examTips: Palmela is the Castelao red anchor within Setubal.

Castelao.summary: Traditional Portuguese red grape producing medium-bodied wines with red fruit, herbs and moderate tannin.
Castelao.style: Red cherry, plum, dried herbs and medium body with moderate tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in sandy or warmer coastal sites
Can handle dry warm conditions
winemaking: Often blended but can make varietal wines
Oak can add structure and spice
examTips: Palmela is the key appellation to connect with Castelao.

palmela.classification: Palmela DOC is the main Castelao-based red appellation of the Setubal peninsula.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Matching grape variety to the peninsula's sandy sites is a major quality factor.

setubal.classification: Setubal combines the fortified Moscatel de Setubal DOC with Palmela DOC for Castelão-based reds.
lawNotes: Moscatel de Setubal is one of Portugal's classic aromatic fortified wines.
Palmela is the red appellation most closely tied to Castelao.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Choice of fortification, sweetness and maturation is a major human factor in regional wine style.
Matching grape variety to site is important across the peninsula's contrasting fortified and still styles.

alentejo.name: Alentejo
alentejo.summary: Hot southern region producing ripe, fruit-forward wines.
examTips: Hot climate → ripe fruit.
Often softer acidity.

Aragonez.summary: Ripe red grape producing fruit-forward wines.
viticulture: Heat tolerant
examTips: Warm climate style.

viticulture: Heat sensitive
examTips: Adds spice.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

madeira.name: Madeira
madeira.summary: Atlantic volcanic island producing long-lived fortified wines with striking acidity and styles ranging from dry to very sweet.
madeira.styleSummary: Fortified wines with high acidity, oxidative complexity and sweetness levels from dry Sercial to rich Malmsey.
examTips: Madeira combines high acidity with fortification and oxidative ageing.
Know the sweetness ladder: Sercial, Verdelho, Bual, Malmsey.
Estufagem and canteiro are the key maturation methods.

Sercial.summary: Classic Madeira grape for the driest style, combining citrus, nuts and very high acidity.
Sercial.style: Dry fortified wine with citrus peel, walnut and smoky oxidative complexity.
viticulture: Often planted at higher elevations
Ripens later and retains acidity strongly
winemaking: Fortified and deliberately heated or oxidatively matured
Usually made in the driest classical style
examTips: Sercial is the driest classical Madeira style.

Verdelho.summary: Madeira grape associated with medium-dry styles showing citrus, dried fruit and smoky complexity.
Verdelho.style: Medium-dry fortified wine with citrus peel, dried fruit and nutty oxidative notes.
viticulture: Suited to warm island conditions while retaining acidity
Often planted in intermediate elevations
examTips: Verdelho sits between dry Sercial and sweeter Bual.

Bual.summary: Madeira grape for medium-sweet styles, combining caramelized richness with balancing acidity.
Bual.style: Medium-sweet fortified wine with toffee, dried fig, coffee and citrus-peel lift.
viticulture: Can achieve fuller ripeness while retaining fresh structure
Often planted in warmer sites than Sercial
examTips: Bual is richer and sweeter than Verdelho, but not as sweet as Malmsey.

Malvasia.summary: Classic Madeira grape for the richest sweetest Malmsey styles, with raisined fruit and caramel complexity.
Malvasia.style: Sweet fortified wine with raisin, toffee, molasses and citrus-peel lift.
viticulture: Usually grown in warmer lower sites
Achieves high sugar ripeness
winemaking: Associated with the sweetest classical Madeira style
examTips: Malmsey is the sweetest classical Madeira category.

sercial-madeira.name: Sercial Madeira
sercial-madeira.style: Dry fortified Madeira with citrus peel, nuts and smoky oxidative complexity.
sercial-madeira.aging: Usually matured oxidatively, often by canteiro in better wines

verdelho-madeira.name: Verdelho Madeira
verdelho-madeira.style: Medium-dry Madeira with dried fruit, citrus peel and nutty complexity.
verdelho-madeira.aging: Oxidative maturation with heat exposure shaping the final style
verdelho-madeira.keyPoint: Medium-dry point on the classical sweetness scale.

bual-madeira.name: Bual Madeira
bual-madeira.style: Medium-sweet Madeira with toffee, dried fig and citrus-peel lift.
bual-madeira.aging: Oxidative ageing and heat build caramelized complexity
bual-madeira.keyPoint: Richer than Verdelho but not the sweetest style.

malmsey-madeira.name: Malmsey Madeira
malmsey-madeira.style: Sweet rich Madeira with raisin, molasses, coffee and vibrant acidity.
malmsey-madeira.aging: Long oxidative maturation creates concentration and exceptional stability

madeira.classification: Madeira DOC is Portugal's classic Atlantic island fortified wine appellation.
lawNotes: Estufagem uses heat treatment, while canteiro is the slower traditional ageing method for better wines.
Madeira's high acidity is the key to balance across dry and sweet styles.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Terraces and steep slopes make exposure and mesoclimate especially important.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Choice of fortification and maturation method is the defining human factor in Madeira style.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
